text.title: Jenkins Pipeline Patterns Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules for production-grade Jenkins pipeline patterns. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Pipelines MUST use Declarative syntax at the top level. Scripted blocks are allowed ONLY inside `script { }` sections with a code comment explaining why Declarative cannot express the logic. Top-level scripted pipelines are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Declarative enforces team readability and enables pre-merge validation via `declarative-linter`. Scripted blocks are opaque to linters, introduce CPS serialization risks, and hide structure from Blue Ocean.

rule.statement: Shared libraries MUST live in a dedicated Git repository separate from application code. Layout: `vars/` (global functions), `src/` (Groovy classes), `resources/` (templates), `test/` (unit tests). All classes MUST implement `java.io.Serializable`. Vars functions MUST NOT override built-in Pipeline steps.
rule.rationale: Dedicated repos enable independent versioning, testing, and rollback. Serializable + the standard layout prevents `NotSerializableException` at CPS checkpoints — the single biggest opaque failure mode in long-running Jenkins builds.

rule.statement: Every `@Library` reference MUST use a pinned tag (`@Library('platform@v1.4.2') _`). Floating `@main` / `@master` refs are forbidden in production pipelines. Implicit library loading MUST be disabled in `Manage Jenkins → Configure System`.
rule.rationale: Floating refs pick the latest commit every build, causing non-deterministic builds. A library bug propagates instantly to all 50+ pipelines with no rollback path. Pinning + explicit imports make the dependency graph auditable.

rule.statement: Parallel branches MUST NOT share the default workspace. Use `dir("branch-${env.STAGE_NAME}")` or distinct `ws()` blocks per branch. Shared resources (DB, credentials, files) MUST be wrapped in `lock(resource: '...')`.
rule.rationale: Jenkins shares workspace across parallel branches by default. Concurrent writes to `target/` or `dist/` cause flaky races that only reproduce under load. `lock()` serialises access to shared external state.

rule.statement: Every `agent { kubernetes { yaml ... } }` container MUST declare both `resources.requests` (CPU + memory) and `resources.limits` (CPU + memory). Privileged containers (docker:dind) MUST be opt-in via explicit `securityContext.privileged: true`.

rationale.source: Jenkins Kubernetes plugin docs + K8s scheduling docs

rule.rationale: Without requests, the scheduler cannot place pods deterministically. Without limits, a runaway build starves the cluster. Privileged-by-default is a security violation; opt-in keeps the blast radius small.

rule.statement: Every pipeline MUST declare `options { timeout(time: 30, unit: 'MINUTES'); buildDiscarder(logRotator(numToKeepStr: '50', artifactNumToKeepStr: '10')); disableConcurrentBuilds(); timestamps(); ansiColor('xterm') }` (or stricter project values). Deploy pipelines touching shared state MUST also use `lock()`.
rule.rationale: Stuck builds without timeout starve the executor queue for hours. Unbounded log retention fills the controller disk. timestamps + ansiColor cut log review time by ~10x.
